summary:
Molars are most affected by severe periodontitis due to their unique anatomy, while other tooth types generally show less pronounced involvement based on current understanding from various medical studies involving individuals with Down syndrome. The primary focus should remain on monitoring these specific areas for early intervention strategies aimed at preventing or managing advanced gum disease effectively. To summarize:

Molars exhibit significant vulnerability to severe periodontitis in people with Down syndrome owing to distinctive dental features that promote bacteria buildup leading to extensive inflammation around the roots. Other teeth typically experience lesser degrees of such complications compared to molars according to existing research findings related to this condition. 

This summary captures the essence of Agent1’s reasoning about why certain teeth are more severely impacted than others when discussing periodontitis risk factors associated with Down syndrome. It highlights critical aspects without unnecessary details not pertinent to answering whether only one type of tooth shows extreme sensitivity towards developing serious forms of gingival diseases under consideration here.
